best_single_bet: Bartholdi, "Automatic actions I" (arXiv:2606.08101). First move (day 1-2): write the Collatz inverse semigroup explicitly as an automatic action — Büchi transducers for both branches of T on L={0,1}^ω (the ×3-carry machine {p,q,r} plus the shift), confirming it satisfies his automatic-action axioms but violates 'bounded' (the carry machine is a strongly-connected nontrivial-cycle component feeding the shift cycle). Then (day 3-5) adapt his Proposition 5.1 pumping argument verbatim-style to prove: THE ORBIT RELATION OF T ON Z_2 IN BASE-2 ENCODING IS NOT ω-REGULAR — using accepted pairs (x, T^k(x)) with x = 1^n0^m tails, where ×3^j stretches blocks at incommensurable rate vs the shift, exactly the synchronization tension his Bernoulli proof exploits. That would upgrade our '≤7-state DFA' certificate theorem to a clean no-go theorem (no ω-regular orbit encoding in base 2 at all), and precisely locates Collatz just past the decidable frontier of a live 2026 research program (his Part II on quantifying over the acting group is announced — contact Bartholdi; note our team's Büchi-nondefinability files suggest partial overlap to reconcile first). Runner-up inside the same week: the Exel–Pardo instantiation with G=BS(1,3) (check pseudo-freeness of the cocycle, then Cor 10.18 + §18 Katsura machinery gives an amenable tight groupoid and computable K-theory for the Collatz algebra — invariants nobody has computed; Mori's Collatz C*-papers don't touch groupoids/K-theory).
dead_ends: (1) My hunch that Willis's scale-groups papers mention 3x+1: FALSE — grep of arXiv:2008.05220 and arXiv:2312.05427 full texts shows zero Collatz/3x+1 content; they are blind ambient geometry only. (2) Nekrashevych's 2025 survey (arXiv:2509.05524): the two '3n+1' grep hits are subscripts w_{3n+1} in a Fibonacci-substitution example — no Collatz content; do not re-search it. (3) Hyperbolic-boundary hyperfiniteness literature (Marquis–Sabok, Naryshkin–Vaes style): does not transfer — our boundary groupoid is not a hyperbolic group boundary; also plain T-orbit hyperfiniteness is trivial anyway (T is conjugate to the one-sided shift, whose tail/orbit relation is classically hyperfinite), so the only non-trivial content is germs/isotropy and the full inverse-semigroup groupoid — route through Exel–Pardo groupoid amenability instead. (4) No dedicated study exists of the semigroup ⟨x→3x, x→x+1, x→2x⟩ on Z_2 as such: closest are BS(1,3)-as-automaton-group papers (found, listed) — the 'nobody computed S_Collatz invariants' claim SURVIVES verification, with three Collatz-AWARE caveats already in our papers/ folder: Mori arXiv:2411.08084 (single-operator/Cuntz formulations, no groupoid/K-theory/self-similar cocycle — verified by grep), Kohl's rcwa work (permutations of Z, not transducer semigroups), Hines (Thompson F). (5) Sutner's research line on rational orbit relations covers only ABELIAN invertible transducers — theorems stop strictly before the non-abelian ×3 carry machine; and his CMU lecture notes explicitly draw the Collatz transducer, so the line is not blind. (6) Contraction question is now CLOSED negatively on the group side: t=×3 satisfies t·0=0, t|_0=t, infinite order, hence t^n|_{0^k}=t^n for all k forces every t^n into any nucleus — the BS(1,3) self-similar action is not contracting (matches the Davis–Elder–Reeves criterion, arXiv:1311.3362); don't spend more time hunting a contracting nucleus for the group completion — the open variant is Nekrashevych's inverse-semigroup/limit-solenoid notion of contraction.

finds.ref: Laurent Bartholdi, "Automatic actions I. Bounded automata and orbits", 2026, arXiv:2606.08101 (preprint, June 2026)
finds.the_theorem: Theorem A: Let a (semi)group / inverse semigroup G act on an ω-regular language L ⊆ Σ^ω by ω-regular transformations (each graph R_g ⊆ L×L accepted by a Büchi transducer), and suppose the action is BOUNDED: each transducer has only finitely many right-infinite paths avoiding 'trivial' states (states from which only diagonal pairs are read) — equivalently no two connected non-trivial cycles. Then the orbit relation O_G is effectively ω-regular. Corollaries B/C: for such actions minimality, topological transitivity, aperiodicity, existence of infinite orbits, infiniteness of G, and every first-order statement over X involving specific elements and 'same orbit' (with cardinality and congruence quantifiers) are DECIDABLE. Proposition 5.1: the two-sided Bernoulli shift admits an automatic presentation whose orbit relation is NOT ω-regular (pumping argument on pairs (…01^n0…, …00^n1^n0…)). Explicitly written in inverse-semigroup language for actions on languages ≠ Σ^ω.
finds.the_match: S_Collatz IS an automatic action of an inverse semigroup in exactly his sense: T's branches x→x/2 (shift on cylinder [0]) and x→(3x+1)/2 (the 3-state carry transducer composed with shift) have ω-regular graphs on base-2 streams; our 'divergence certificate = invariant regular set' framing is the invariant-set shadow of his orbit-relation ω-regularity. WHERE IT'S LOOSE: the boundedness hypothesis FAILS for Collatz — the ×3 carry machine (states n→3n, 3n+1, 3n+2) is strongly connected with non-trivial cycles feeding the shift cycle, so Theorem A does not apply; every path avoids trivial states. Also his framework has no quantification over the acting semigroup (no '∃g' — so 'some iterate reaches 1' is a fixed-x infinitary statement handled only through the orbit relation itself). His inspiration paper (Bondarenko–Wächter arXiv:1912.06897, also blind: DFA description of sequences with infinite orbit under BOUNDED automata, deciding finiteness and level-transitivity) marks the same frontier.
finds.transfer: Two concrete statements: (1) Prove: the orbit relation of T on Z_2 in the standard base-2 encoding is not ω-regular — by adapting his Prop 5.1 pumping argument to pairs (x, T^k x); this generalizes our ≤7-state-DFA certificate nonexistence to a full no-go theorem for Büchi-recognizable orbit structure. (2) Prove or refute: NO ω-regular encoding of Z_2 makes the Collatz action bounded — a machine-checkable formulation of the base-2/base-3 incompatibility (in base 3 the ×3 branch is bounded but /2 is not; quantify the obstruction as 'two connected non-trivial cycles are unavoidable'). If instead some exotic encoding were bounded, his Corollary B would make divergence/cycle questions decidable — so either outcome is a theorem about Collatz hardness.

finds.ref: Ruy Exel and Enrique Pardo, "Self-similar graphs, a unified treatment of Katsura and Nekrashevych C*-algebras", 2017, Advances in Mathematics 306, 1046–1129 (arXiv:1409.1107)
finds.the_theorem: Given a countable discrete group G acting on a finite graph E without sources, with a 1-cocycle φ: G×E^1→G (standing hypothesis (2.3)), they build an inverse semigroup S_{G,E} and prove: O_{G,E} ≅ C*_tight(S_{G,E}) ≅ C*(G_tight(S_{G,E})). Cor 10.16: G amenable ⇒ O_{G,E} nuclear. Cor 10.18: G amenable ⇒ G_tight(S_{G,E}) is an AMENABLE étale groupoid (and if (G,E,φ) is pseudo-free, so is its sibling G_{G,E}). Thm 16.1: for amenable G (plus (2.3)), O_{G,E} is simple iff explicitly listed conditions on the action (minimality + faithfulness-type conditions on germs) hold; Cor 16.3: Hausdorff + simple ⇒ purely infinite. §18: Katsura triples = G=Z with affine cocycles t|_e = t^b given by integer matrices (A,B) — i.e., exactly affine letter-by-letter transducers — with K-theory computable from (A,B) (O_{A,B} realize all Kirchberg algebras).
finds.the_match: Exact instantiation: E = one vertex with two loops (binary alphabet), G = BS(1,3) = ⟨a: x↦x+1, t: x↦3x⟩ acting self-similarly on {0,1}^* (wreath recursion a = σ(1,a); t = (t, at) — the 3-state carry machine), φ(g,e) = the section. G is solvable, hence amenable. The forward Collatz map decomposes as T = s_0* ⊔ (a²t)s_1* inside S_{G,E} (even branch: delete prefix 0; odd branch x=2y+1 ↦ 3y+2 = a²t(y) after deleting prefix 1), so S_Collatz is an inverse subsemigroup of S_{G,E}. GAPS: (i) pseudo-freeness of the BS(1,3) cocycle must be checked (needed for the sibling groupoid and parts of simplicity); (ii) amenability of the ambient groupoid gives measurewise amenability/hyperfiniteness of the restricted S_Collatz orbit relation, but BOREL hyperfiniteness does not follow; (iii) S_Collatz's own tight groupoid vs. the restriction of G_tight(S_{G,E}) may differ — the ambient groupoid's orbits (which include +1) are strictly coarser than Collatz forward-backward orbits, so minimality of the ambient groupoid does NOT decide Collatz; the certificate question lives in the sub-groupoid.
finds.transfer: Compute, with proofs: (1) G_tight(S_{BS(1,3),E}) is amenable and O nuclear (immediate from Cor 10.18 since BS(1,3) amenable) ⇒ the germ groupoid of the Collatz transduction on Z_2 is measure-amenable, and its orbit relation is μ-hyperfinite for every quasi-invariant μ — answering our hyperfiniteness question at the measurable level; (2) write the ×3+1 recursion as a Katsura triple (A,B integer matrix data from t=(t,at), a=σ(1,a)) and compute K_0/K_1 of the Collatz algebra from §18 — a brand-new computable invariant; (3) reinterpret divergence certificates as open invariant subsets of the unit space under the SUB-groupoid generated by T's branches, and port Thm 16.1's minimality conditions into checkable statements about which invariant subsets can be 'tight' (regular/sofic). Novelty verified: Mori's Collatz C*-papers (arXiv:2411.08084) use none of this machinery.

finds.ref: Rostislav Grigorchuk and Dmytro Savchuk, "Self-similar groups acting essentially freely on the boundary of the binary rooted tree", 2013, arXiv:1212.0605
finds.the_theorem: Theorem 1.1: among ALL groups generated by 3-state automata over the 2-letter alphabet, the complete list of those acting essentially freely (trivial stabilizers off a null set, w.r.t. the uniform measure on ∂T_2) is given; it includes the Baumslag–Solitar groups BS(1,3) = ⟨t,x | t^x = t^3⟩ (automata #870, #924) and BS(1,−3) (#2294, #2320), the free abelian groups, and specified extensions (e.g., BS(1,3)⋊Z/2). The BS(1,3) realization is the wreath recursion a=(c,b)σ, b=(a,c), c=(b,a), i.e., exactly the odometer + multiplication-by-3-with-carry action on Z_2.
finds.the_match: This is literally 'who studies the group generated by x→3x and x→x+1 on Z_2': BS(1,3) with its ±carry-state automaton IS that group, and the paper proves its boundary action on (Z_2, Haar) is essentially free. Match is exact for the GROUP part of S_Collatz. LOOSE: essential freeness concerns the group of units, not the non-invertible branch maps (2x is injective-not-surjective; T is 2-to-1); the measure is Haar on Z_2, while integer orbits are a null set — statements are a.e., silent about Z ⊂ Z_2.
finds.transfer: Combine essential freeness with amenability of BS(1,3): the boundary action generates (a.e.) the hyperfinite orbit equivalence relation of an amenable group, so the germ isotropy of the full Collatz groupoid is a.e. trivial — the von Neumann algebra of the ambient groupoid is the injective factor of an equivalence relation, and all measure-theoretic invariants of the Collatz transduction (Radon–Nikodym cocycle = the log-drift (log3 − 2log2 for accelerated T), Krieger type of the associated flow) become computable. Concrete statement to prove: identify the Krieger type of the measured groupoid of S_Collatz on (Z_2, Haar) with the drift cocycle — type III_λ with λ determined by 3/4 — turning the 'negative log-drift heuristic' into a classified ergodic-theoretic invariant.

finds.ref: George A. Willis, "Scale groups", 2020-24, arXiv:2008.05220; and Rostislav Grigorchuk, Dmytro Savchuk, "Liftable self-similar groups and scale groups", 2023-24, arXiv:2312.05427
finds.the_theorem: Willis: closed vertex-transitive subgroups of Isom(T_{q+1}) fixing an end of the regular tree ('scale groups') correspond bijectively (i) to closed SELF-REPLICATING groups acting on rooted trees (vertex stabilizers project onto the original group), and (ii) to elements of positive scale in totally disconnected locally compact groups (scale function/tidy subgroup theory). Grigorchuk–Savchuk: a self-replicating group L admitting a 'lifting' monomorphism σ (satisfying π∘σ = id on sections) embeds via ascending HNN extension into the end-stabilizer of Aut(T̃_{d+1}); Example 5.5: BS(1,3) with the automaton recursion a=(c,b)ε, b=(a,c), c=(b,a) is liftable, self-replicating, and its HNN closure is a scale group; the ambient identification is with subgroups of D(Q_2) (affine-type groups on the Bruhat–Tits tree of Q_2).
finds.the_match: The Collatz dynamics naturally lives on the (2+1)-regular tree with a fixed end (= Q_2 with the ×2 shift toward the end): /2 is the end-translation, ×3 the unit twist preserving horocycles (= levels = 2-adic valuation shells), +1 the horospherical part. Scale groups are exactly the closed ambient symmetry groups of this picture, and BS(1,3)'s liftability puts ⟨+1, ×3⟩ + the ×2 translation inside one classified tdlc scale group. LOOSE (flag hard): T is NOT a group element — it is an element of the topological full group/pseudogroup of the scale-group action (a piecewise choice between two end-translations with clopen pieces {evens},{odds}); scale theory has no off-the-shelf theorems about individual full-group elements' orbits; and both papers prove correspondence/embedding theorems, not orbit theorems.
finds.transfer: Concrete computations to attempt: (1) compute the scale function s(g) and tidy subgroups for g = the closure of the two Collatz branch isometries in the scale group P ≤ Aut(T_3) containing the lifted BS(1,3) — the scale is the structural home of the 3-vs-4 drift (modular function of the groupoid); (2) use Willis's flat-group machinery to classify the closed P-invariant and ⟨branches⟩-invariant subsets of the horospherical boundary, asking whether any avoids the orbit of 1 — a topological-group-theoretic reformulation of divergence certificates one level above regular sets; (3) ask Willis-school experts whether the topological full group of a scale group has been studied at all (it appears not — genuinely open territory).

finds.ref: Daniele D'Angeli, Emanuele Rodaro, Jan Philipp Wächter, "On the structure theory of partial automaton semigroups", 2020, Semigroup Forum 101:51–76 (arXiv:1811.09420); and A. Olijnyk, V. Sushchansky, J. Słupik, "Inverse semigroups of partial automaton permutations", 2010, IJAC 20(7):923–952
finds.the_theorem: D'Angeli–Rodaro–Wächter: for semigroups generated by PARTIAL letter-to-letter transducers: (a) the class equals the class of complete-automaton semigroups iff the latter is closed under removing an adjoined zero (open problem of Cain); (b) no semidirect product with a nontrivial subsemigroup of the free monogenic semigroup is an automaton semigroup; (c) every inverse automaton semigroup is generated by an invertible-but-partial automaton ('automaton-inverse semigroups' are the right category). Olijnyk–Sushchansky–Słupik: the inverse semigroup of ALL partial automaton permutations over a finite alphabet is characterized via wreath products; Green's relations and the conjugacy relation are described explicitly.
finds.the_match: S_Collatz is an inverse semigroup generated by partial invertible transductions of {0,1}^ω — the category these papers axiomatize. Exact for the 'horizontal' part: every restriction of g ∈ BS(1,3) to a cylinder is a partial automaton permutation, and their wreath-product coordinates + Green's relations apply verbatim. LOOSE (flag): their automata are LETTER-TO-LETTER (level-preserving), while T's branches shift levels (delete/prepend a digit); so S_Collatz = (their category) ⋈ polycyclic monoid P_2 (Cuntz part), and the composite is strictly outside their theorems. This is precisely where inverse-semigroup theory is thinner than group theory: there is no partial-automaton analogue of the nucleus/contraction toolkit yet.
finds.transfer: Prove a presentation: S_Collatz ≅ ⟨P_2 (prefix codes) together with the partial automaton permutations of BS(1,3)-sections⟩ with explicit relations, and compute its Green's relations / maximal subgroups using Olijnyk–Sushchansky–Słupik coordinates — the first 'basic invariants of S_Collatz' deliverable, publishable in the semigroup community. Then use D'Angeli–Rodaro–Wächter's (c) to normalize S_Collatz's generating automaton to a canonical invertible-partial form, giving a well-defined finite presentation on which finiteness/orbit questions can be posed to their decidability program.

finds.ref: Volodymyr Nekrashevych, "Self-similar inverse semigroups and Smale spaces", 2006, IJAC 16(5):849–874; updated exposition in Nekrashevych, "Semigroups, groups, and algebras of dynamical origin", 2025, arXiv:2509.05524 (survey, §7 limit solenoids); cf. Deeley–Yashinski, "Self-similar inverse semigroups from Wieler solenoids", 2020, Mathematics (MDPI) 8(2):266
finds.the_theorem: A CONTRACTING self-similar inverse semigroup (finite set N such that restrictions of long compositions of generators to deep enough vertices land in N) has a limit solenoid: a Smale-type dynamical system on which the semigroup's germ groupoid acts as the unstable/holonomy groupoid; conversely every irreducible Smale space with totally disconnected stable sets (Wieler solenoid) arises this way from the adjacency inverse semigroup of a Markov partition (via s-resolving factor maps — Deeley–Yashinski), with tight groupoid equivalent to the unstable groupoid. Periodic points of the solenoid correspond to explicit finite nucleus data; the survey works out limit dynamical systems of shift-invariant groupoids in full detail.
finds.the_match: The Collatz backward branches {x↦2x, x↦(2x−1)/3} form an IFS on Z_2 whose images are exactly the depth-1 cylinders (evens/odds) — precisely the adjacency-semigroup data of a Markov partition, with sections in BS(1,3); the natural candidate 'limit solenoid' is a 3x+1 mapping torus whose periodic points would parametrize Collatz cycles over Z_2 (including the known rational cycles). WHERE IT BREAKS (flag): contraction FAILS on the group side — t=×3 has t·0=0, t|_0=t, infinite order, so t^n|_{0^k}=t^n forever and any nucleus must contain all t^n (this settles 'is S_Collatz contracting, what is its nucleus' NEGATIVELY for the group completion; consistent with the Davis–Elder–Reeves non-contraction criterion, arXiv:1311.3362). Whether S_Collatz is contracting in Nekrashevych's INVERSE-SEMIGROUP sense (which quantifies only over compositions that occur as partial maps, not over the whole group) is genuinely open — the partial-map theory is thinner and the definition is sensitive to which restrictions exist.
finds.transfer: Decide: is S_Collatz contracting as a self-similar inverse semigroup (Nekrashevych's Def. in IJAC 2006 / survey §7)? If YES: construct its limit solenoid, identify the shift's periodic points with 3x+1 cycles, and read cycle-rigidity statements from the Smale-space structure (e.g., zeta-function finiteness ⇒ finiteness statements about cycles of bounded combinatorics). If NO: prove the failure is forced by the 2-3 carry interaction — a new formalization of base-2/base-3 incompatibility parallel to the Bartholdi boundedness failure. Either branch is a definite theorem about our object.

finds.ref: Pierre Gillibert, "The finiteness problem for automaton semigroups is undecidable", 2014, IJAC 24(1):1–9 (arXiv:1304.2295); Daniele D'Angeli, Emanuele Rodaro, Jan Philipp Wächter, "Automaton semigroups and groups: on the undecidability of problems related to freeness and finiteness", 2020, Israel J. Math (arXiv:1712.07408); Ievgen Bondarenko, Jan Philipp Wächter, "On orbits and the finiteness of bounded automaton groups", 2021, IJAC (arXiv:1912.06897)
finds.the_theorem: Gillibert: from any NW-deterministic Wang tile set one constructs a Mealy automaton whose generated semigroup is infinite iff the plane admits a valid tiling; with Kari's undecidability theorem, finiteness of automaton semigroups is undecidable; the order problem for automaton (semi)groups is likewise undecidable (Gillibert 2018). D'Angeli–Rodaro–Wächter: freeness and related problems undecidable; word problem PSPACE-complete for automaton (semi)groups. Bondarenko–Wächter (positive side): for BOUNDED automata (Sidki class), the set of sequences with infinite orbit is recognized by an explicit DFA, so finiteness and level-transitivity are decidable.
finds.the_match: Our program's computational face — 'compute basic invariants of S_Collatz', 'search for regular invariant sets' — lives exactly on the frontier these theorems chart: general transducer-semigroup invariants are Π/Σ-hard via tilings, while the bounded/abelian subclasses are decidable with DFA-described orbit sets (the exact technology of our 7-state certificate search). The Collatz transducer sits in the gap: affine (structured, so generic undecidability doesn't apply) but unbounded in Sidki's hierarchy (the ×3 machine has full activity growth, so the positive theorems don't apply either). LOOSE: none of these results is about a fixed single automaton — they calibrate method difficulty, not Collatz itself.
finds.transfer: Two uses: (1) calibration theorem to prove — 'the uniform divergence-certificate problem (given a k-state transducer of Collatz type, does a regular invariant set avoiding 1 exist?) is undecidable', by splicing Gillibert's Wang-tile gadget into the affine carry-machine family; this would explain structurally why our certificate search must be bespoke; (2) mine the Bondarenko–Wächter DFA-of-infinite-orbits construction for the exact automaton-theoretic obstruction when activity is unbounded — their proof is the template Bartholdi generalized, and its failure mode on the ×3 machine is a specific lemma to attack (which sequences have DFA-recognizable orbit-growth profiles under affine unbounded transducers?).

finds.ref: Klaus Sutner and Kevin Lewi, "Iterating invertible binary transducers", 2012, J. Automata, Languages and Combinatorics 17(2-4):293–313; Klaus Sutner, "Abelian invertible automata" (in Reversibility and Universality, 2018) and "Invertible transducers, iteration and coordinates" (CIAA 2013)
finds.the_theorem: For the class of invertible binary transducers whose transduction semigroup is ABELIAN: the group is free abelian (or boolean); orbits of finite words are affine subspaces in an explicit coordinate geometry ('principal functions', 2-adic algebraic); for a subclass the ITERATED transduction relation (x, g^k(x) for some k) is RATIONAL, with complexity bounds for natural orbit problems. (Blindness caveat, checked honestly: Sutner's CMU lecture notes (CDM 'Mealy Machines', 2025) explicitly construct the Collatz transducer — the 3-state machine q:n↦3n+2, p:n↦3n+1, r:n↦3n plus a division-by-2 top part — and remark that iteration 'can produce very complicated results'; I could not obtain the JALC paper's text to verify it is Collatz-free, so I mark the line not-blind.)
finds.the_match: The ×3-with-carry machine is an invertible binary transducer in exactly his class of objects, and 'orbit relation of the iterated transduction is rational' is exactly the kind of statement our divergence-certificate program needs. THE GAP IS SHARP: his theorems require the transduction group to be abelian; the carry states {3n, 3n+1, 3n+2} do not commute (they generate a metabelian BS(1,3)-type group), so every Sutner theorem misses Collatz by precisely the non-commutativity of carries. Also his orbit results iterate a SINGLE transduction g, whereas T mixes two branches chosen by parity.
finds.transfer: Extend Sutner's coordinate/principal-function machinery from free-abelian to metabelian transduction groups: concretely, decide whether the iterated-orbit relation {(x, t^k(x)) : k ∈ N} of the ×3 machine t on {0,1}^* is rational (t alone, before mixing with the shift). If rational, combine with the /2 shift via closure properties to bound where T-orbit rationality first fails; if not rational, that pinpoints carries (not branch-mixing) as the first obstruction. Sutner is at CMU — same institution as this program — a conversation, not just a citation.

signature: (Inverse) semigroups generated by letter-by-letter transducers acting on rooted trees / ω-words: self-similar and automaton (semi)groups, contraction/nucleus, ω-regular orbit relations, boundary germ groupoids and their amenability — hunted without ever searching 'Collatz'/'3x+1'.
